perturbation.original_text: Deposit. A 10% escrow d eposit will be required upon signing of a Purchase and Sale Agreement and will be held by Broker in a non -interest bearing account.
perturbation.changed_text: Deposit. An escrow deposit will be required upon signing of a Purchase and Sale Agreement and will be held by Broker.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific deposit percentage and the detail about the account not bearing interest creates an omission that could lead to disputes and potential violations of Massachusetts law regarding escrow accounts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law (M.G.L. c. 255, § 12I) regulates escrow accounts and requires clarity regarding the handling of funds. Omitting the specific deposit percentage and whether it's interest-bearing introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting the law's intent to protect the buyer's deposit and to provide clear terms.
perturbation.justification: The altered contract omits essential information regarding the deposit amount and whether the escrow account bears interest. The Perturbation Explanation states that this omission may violate Massachusetts law concerning escrow accounts. The Law Explanation reinforces this, stating that the omission introduces ambiguity that may contradict the law's intent to protect the buyer's deposit and provide clear terms.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer agrees that s/he will not engage with any Listing Party at any time subsequent to being introduced by Corbett for the purpose of purchasing a Listing without Broker’s involvement.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that s/he will not engage with any Listing Party for the purpose of purchasing a Listing without Broker’s involvement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'at any time subsequent to being introduced by Corbett' weakens the clause and allows for circumvention after a certain undefined period, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 93A prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in trade or commerce. By removing 'at any time subsequent to being introduced by Corbett', the modified text allows a buyer to potentially circumvent the broker's involvement after an initial introduction. This could be deemed an unfair practice if it deprives the broker of their commission earned through the initial introduction, potentially violating Chapter 93A.

perturbation.justification: The omission of "at any time subsequent to being introduced by Corbett" weakens the original text, potentially allowing the buyer to circumvent the broker's involvement after the initial introduction. The Law Explanation mentions that this could be deemed an unfair practice if it deprives the broker of their commission, potentially violating Chapter 93A. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Chapter 93A, which regulates business practices for consumers protection. The modified contract now allows an undefined period where the buyer can circumvent the broker, creating a legal loophole by omitting the time restriction and therefore contradicting the law because it deprives the broker of commission.

perturbation.original_text: Understanding that, Buyer shall perform its own due diligence prior to entering into an agreement to purchase any Listing . Buyer agrees that Broker is not responsible for the accuracy or completeness of any of the information Buyer receives or fails to receive, and B uyer agrees to, defend , indemnify and hold harmless Broker and any of its agents from any claims or damages which may occur by reason of the inaccuracy or incompleteness of any information provided to Buyer with respect to any business Buyer might purchase .
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer agrees that Broker is not responsible for the accuracy or completeness of any of the information Buyer receives or fails to receive, and B uyer agrees to, defend , indemnify and hold harmless Broker and any of its agents from any claims or damages which may occur by reason of the inaccuracy or incompleteness of any information provided to Buyer with respect to any business Buyer might purchase .
perturbation.explanation: By removing the explicit 'Buyer shall perform its own due diligence prior to entering into an agreement to purchase any Listing' clause, the burden on the broker increases and could lead to legal issues if the broker provides inaccurate information, potentially contradicting Massachusetts consumer protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act, M.G.L. c. 93A, §§ 2(a)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act (M.G.L. c. 93A, §§ 2(a)) prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in trade or commerce. While the agreement states the broker is not responsible for inaccurate information, removing the clause emphasizing the buyer's due diligence could imply the broker bears more responsibility. This is a gray area, but if the broker provides misleading information (even unintentionally), the omission of the due diligence requirement could expose them to liability under 93A if a court finds the broker's actions to be deceptive, due to the imbalance of information and the obscured expectation that the buyer would do their part in verification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. Such rules and regulations shall not be inconsistent with the rules, regulations and decisions of the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts interpreting the provisions of 15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1) (The Federal Trade Commission Act), as from time to time amended. × Register for MyLegislature Register With An Existing Account Sign in with Facebook Sign in with Google Register With Your Email Address Email Address Password Confirm Password Register Registering... perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'Buyer shall perform its own due diligence' clause increases the broker's potential liability under Massachusetts Consumer Protection Act (M.G.L. c. 93A, §§ 2(a)). By removing this clause, the broker's responsibility is amplified, potentially creating an unfair or deceptive act if the broker provides inaccurate information, even unintentionally. This could violate Section 2(a) of Chapter 93A, which states that 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful.' The modified text contradicts the law by potentially enabling a scenario where the broker's actions, in the absence of a buyer's due diligence obligation, could be interpreted as deceptive, thus undermining the consumer protection mandate.
